prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. This is from my colleague who teaches writing at the local university. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. This is from my colleague who teaches writing at the local university. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

**Grade: 8/10**

Here is the feedback from the perspective of a university writing instructor.

### What Works
*   **Clear Narrative Arc:** The essay has a solid structure that moves logically from the setup (driveway confidence) to the conflict (tryouts reality), the climax (the cut list), and the resolution (joining debate).
*   **Authentic Voice:** The tone is conversational and honest. It sounds like a real student reflecting on their life, which makes it engaging to read. The admission that Marcus made the team adds a nice layer of vulnerability and realism.
*   **Strong Insight:** The reflection in the second-to-last paragraph is excellent. Specifically, the realization that "you don’t know what you don’t know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious" is a sophisticated observation about the Dunning-Kruger effect (even if they didn't name it). That specific observation saves the essay from being a generic "I worked hard and won" story.

### What Doesn’t Work (Room for Improvement)
*   **"Show, Don’t Tell":** There is a bit too much summarizing and not enough scene-setting. For example, the phrase "I was really upset for a while" is a missed opportunity. *Show* the reader the upset: Did you rip the list off the wall? Did you sit in the locker room for an hour? Specific sensory details would make the emotional impact land harder.
*   **Sentence Variety:** Many sentences follow a similar Subject-Verb pattern ("I tried," "I realized," "I thought"). Combining some sentences and using subordination (using words like although, while, despite) would make the prose flow more smoothly and sound more mature.
*   **Length and Depth:** The essay appears to be a bit under the 500-word target (it looks to be around 350-400 words). Because it is short, the transition to the debate team feels slightly rushed. Expanding on *why* debate filled the void left by basketball would strengthen the resolution.
*   **The Conclusion:** The final sentence ("failure isn't the end... it's just information") is a bit of a cliché. Ending on the previous sentence about "you don't know what you don't know" would have been much stronger and more unique to the writer's voice.

**Overall:** This is a competent, readable essay with a strong thematic core. With a bit more descriptive imagery and deeper exploration of the transition to debate, it could easily be a 9 or 10.

My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. This is from my colleague who teaches writing at the local university. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. This is from my colleague who teaches writing at the local university. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. This is from my colleague who teaches writing at the local university. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

Here is a breakdown of the essay based on university writing standards.

**Grade: 8/10**

This is a solid, competent narrative. It succeeds because it is honest, structures the story clearly, and arrives at a mature insight without overstating its case. However, it falls short of a higher grade because it feels slightly rushed in the second half and relies too heavily on "telling" rather than "showing" regarding the solution (joining the debate team).

### What Works
*   **Authentic Voice:** The narrator sounds like a real person. The conversational tone ("Obviously," "Turns out") works well for a personal narrative, making the failure feel relatable rather than melodramatic.
*   **Clear Narrative Arc:** The essay has a distinct beginning (false confidence), middle (the reality check during tryouts), and end (the lesson learned). The transition from the driveway to the gym effectively sets up the conflict.
*   **Specific Details:** The basketball section is strong because of specific terminology ("pick and roll," "fast break drill") and the specific moment of shame (being told to "stay out of the way"). These ground the reader in the experience.
*   **Mature Insight:** The conclusion avoids clichés. The realization that "casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good" and the acknowledgment of the Dunning-Kruger effect ("you don't know what you don't know") show a high level of self-awareness for a short essay.

### What Doesn't Work (Room for Improvement)
*   **The "Pivot" is Rushed:** The essay spends roughly 75% of the word count on the failure and only 25% on the recovery. The transition to the debate team happens very fast ("sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me"). To improve this, the writer needs to slow down and show *why* debate was a good fit. Was it the adrenaline of competition? The strategy? As written, it feels like a plot device rather than a genuine discovery.
*   **Length and Pacing:** The prompt asked for approximately 500 words, and this essay comes in around 350–400 words. It feels slightly underdeveloped. Expanding on the debate section would not only meet the word count but also strengthen the thematic link between "arguing" and "competing."
*   **Showing vs. Telling (The Lesson):** The writer tells us debate was "one of the best experiences of high school," but doesn't give an example. A single specific detail about a debate win or a moment of feeling "in the zone" during a debate would parallel the basketball tryouts and make the "learning" part of the prompt much stronger.
*   **Informal Closing:** The phrase "So yeah" in the final paragraph is too casual for a university-level submission. It undermines the authority of the final insight.

**Summary:** This is a "B" paper. It is a pleasant read and answers the prompt, but it plays it safe. To get an "A," the writer needs to dig deeper into the "what I learned" section by expanding on the debate team experience with the same level of detail used for the basketball tryouts.

Here is a grade and feedback for the essay, written from the perspective of a university writing instructor.

**Grade: 7/10**

**What Works:**
*   **Clear Narrative Arc:** The essay has a solid beginning, middle, and end. It establishes the stakes (making the team), introduces a conflict (the reality of skill gap), and resolves with a clear pivot (joining debate) and a lesson learned.
*   **Honesty and Voice:** The voice feels authentic. The admission of thinking they were better than they were because they could beat their friend in the driveway is a relatable and specific detail that grounds the story.
*   **The "So What?":** The conclusion avoids being too generic. Specifically, the distinction between "doing something for fun" vs. "doing it competitively" is a sophisticated observation for a short narrative. The realization that "you don't know what you don't know" is also a strong insight.
*   **Specific Details:** Mentioning the "pick and roll" and the specific line "just stay out of the way" adds necessary texture to the story.

**What Doesn’t Work (Areas for Improvement):**
*   **Show, Don’t Tell:** The essay relies too heavily on summarizing feelings rather than depicting them. For example, the phrase "I was really upset for a while" tells the reader the emotion. To improve, the writer should describe the physical sensation of that moment—walking to the list, the sinking stomach, the silence on the car ride home.
*   **Sentence Variety:** Many sentences follow a similar subject-verb structure ("I thought," "I realized," "I found out"). Combining sentences or using introductory clauses would make the writing flow more smoothly and feel less repetitive.
*   **Length and Depth:** The essay is likely under the 500-word target (it appears to be around 350-400 words). To reach the full potential of the prompt, the writer could expand on the *debate* section. We are told debate was a "better fit," but we don't see a moment of success in debate to contrast with the failure in basketball.
*   **Casual Conclusion:** The phrase "So yeah" is too colloquial for a formal essay. While a conversational tone is fine, the final sentence should land with more authority to leave a lasting impression.
